mnemonics.title: Kruskal's Steps
mnemonics.content:
S - SORT edges by weight
A - ADD edges one by one
S - SKIP if cycle forms
S - STOP when V-1 edges selected
MEMORIZE: 'SASS' - SORT, ADD, SKIP, STOP

mnemonics.title: Union-Find Operations
mnemonics.content:
FIND the root (representative)
UNION two sets together
MEMORIZE: 'FUN' - Find rep, Union, Number of sets decreases

acronyms.usage: The tree with minimum total edge weight connecting all vertices
Also called Union-Find, the data structure used for cycle detection
Used in time complexity O(E log E)
Amortized time complexity of optimized Union-Find operations

visualTips.visualization: Imagine sorting coins (edges) by their weight from lightest to heaviest, then picking coins one by one while ensuring you don't pick a coin that completes a circle pattern.
Picture each vertex as a separate tree initially. When you select an edge connecting two trees, you 'merge' them into one larger tree. The FIND operation traces up to find the tree root.
Imagine building a bridge network. You need exactly (V-1) bridges to connect V cities without creating loops - that's your stop signal.
Think of vertices as islands and edges as bridges. Adding a bridge between two islands already connected by water would create a cycle (a loop).

analogies.analogy: Like building a railway network by always laying the cheapest possible track that doesn't create a loop - choosing locally optimal at each step leads to globally optimal network.
Like tracking which students are in which group during a school activity. FIND checks which group a student belongs to, UNION merges two groups into one.
Like choosing which friendships to form by starting with the strongest bonds (lowest weight edges) while avoiding creating exclusive cliques (cycles).
Like the 'cheapest bridge' rule in city planning - whichever bridge is the cheapest way to connect two disconnected areas should definitely be in the final road network.
